problems.problem: A concrete bridge span carries heavy traffic loads that put its underside in tension as it bends. Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension, so under service loads the lower fibres of the span crack, and its real load-bearing strength falls well short of what the cross-section should be able to provide. We need the span to withstand large bending loads without the concrete cracking on its tension side.
problems.source: casebase.json 012 (TRIZ textbook); original source in notes

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables through ducts running lengthwise in the concrete bridge span. Before any traffic uses the bridge, stretch these cables to very high tension using hydraulic jacks and anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. This creates enormous forces that squeeze the entire concrete span into compression, especially along the bottom surface that will later face tension from traffic.

When vehicles cross the bridge and cause it to bend, the bottom of the span tries to stretch and crack. However, the pre-installed compression from the tensioned cables counteracts this stretching force. The traffic loads reduce the compression but don't eliminate it, so the concrete never actually experiences tension—it stays compressed or at worst reaches zero stress. Since the concrete never goes into tension, it never cracks.

This works because we've essentially pre-loaded the structure with forces opposite to those it will experience in service. The steel cables remain permanently stretched, continuously pushing the concrete together, which prevents the tension cracks that would otherwise form when traffic bends the span.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking on its underside under tension from heavy traffic, steel reinforcing bars are embedded within the concrete along the lower portion of the span. These bars are positioned where the tension forces are greatest. As the bridge bends under load, the concrete on the bottom tries to stretch but is restrained by the steel, which is excellent at resisting tension. The concrete itself remains in compression on the top side, where it is strongest. The two materials work together because the rough surface of the steel bars bonds securely with the concrete, and they expand and contract at similar rates with temperature changes. This combination creates a strong, composite beam that fully utilizes the concrete's compressive strength and the steel's tensile strength, allowing the span to support heavy loads without the concrete cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete from cracking under tension, we will embed high-strength steel strands within the lower portion of the bridge span. These strands will be tensioned *before* the concrete is poured and allowed to cure around them. Once the concrete has hardened, the tension in the strands will be gradually released. As the strands try to shorten, they will compress the surrounding concrete. This pre-compression will effectively "pre-stress" the concrete, introducing an internal compressive force that counteracts the tensile forces generated by traffic loads. When the bridge is subjected to bending, the external tensile forces will first have to overcome this built-in compression before the concrete itself experiences any tension. This ensures the concrete remains in compression or at least at zero tension, preventing cracks and allowing the full cross-section to contribute to load bearing.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we will use steel reinforcement. Steel reinforcing bars, or rebar, will be embedded within the concrete, particularly in the lower portion of the span where tension forces are most significant. This combination of materials creates a composite structure that utilizes the compressive strength of concrete and the tensile strength of steel. The rebar will carry the tensile loads that the concrete cannot, preventing cracks from forming under heavy traffic loads. Additionally, we can employ pre-stressed concrete techniques, where steel tendons are tensioned before the concrete is poured. This method keeps the concrete in compression even when the bridge is under load, further enhancing its ability to resist tensile forces. This solution effectively increases the load-bearing capacity of the bridge span, ensuring it can withstand large bending loads without cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text:
Before the bridge carries any traffic, install strong steel cables or tendons through hollow ducts running lengthwise through the concrete span. Once the concrete has hardened completely, use powerful hydraulic jacks to stretch these cables tight, then anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. 

When you pull the cables tight, they try to shorten, but being anchored, they instead squeeze the entire concrete span lengthwise, creating compression forces throughout the beam - particularly along the bottom surface where cracking would normally occur. 

Now when heavy vehicles cross the bridge and cause it to bend downward, the bottom of the span experiences a reduction in compression rather than going into harmful tension. The pre-compressed concrete can handle the bending because it stays in compression, which concrete handles excellently. The bottom surface never reaches the tension state that would cause cracking, so the full thickness of concrete contributes to carrying the load. This technique, widely used in modern bridge construction, allows longer spans and heavier loads than ordinary reinforced concrete.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: The solution is to embed high-strength steel bars, known as reinforcement, into the lower part of the concrete bridge span where tension occurs during bending. When traffic loads bend the bridge, the bottom surface experiences stretching forces. Instead of relying on the weak concrete alone to resist this tension, the force is transferred to the steel bars inside. The steel, being very strong in tension, stretches slightly without breaking and carries the tensile load. This keeps the concrete from being overstressed and cracking, allowing the entire cross-section to contribute to strength. The concrete continues to handle the compression at the top, while the steel handles the tension at the bottom. This combination makes the bridge span much stronger and more durable under heavy loads, as both materials work together according to their inherent strengths.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking on its underside, we can embed high-strength steel cables or bars within the concrete along its bottom edge. Before the bridge is put into service, these steel elements are stretched very tightly using powerful jacks and then anchored securely at the ends of the span. Once anchored, the jacks are released, and the stretched steel tries to shorten, pulling the concrete around it into a state of compression. This means that even before any traffic crosses the bridge, the bottom of the span is already being squeezed together. When heavy vehicles then drive over the bridge, the bending forces try to pull the bottom of the span apart. However, these pulling forces must first overcome the pre-existing squeezing force from the steel, effectively canceling out the tension and preventing the concrete from cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking under tension, we can incorporate a system of pre-stressed steel cables within the concrete structure. These steel cables are placed in ducts within the concrete and are tensioned before the concrete is poured. Once the concrete has cured, the tension in the cables is released, which compresses the concrete. This pre-compression counteracts the tensile forces that occur when the bridge is under load. By doing so, the concrete remains in compression even when the bridge is subjected to heavy traffic loads, preventing cracks from forming on the tension side. Additionally, using high-strength steel cables ensures that the system can handle significant loads without failure. This method effectively increases the load-bearing capacity of the bridge span, allowing it to withstand large bending loads without compromising the structural integrity of the concrete. The combination of concrete's compressive strength and the tensile strength of steel provides a robust solution to the problem.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or tendons through ducts cast into the lower portion of the concrete bridge span. Before the bridge opens to traffic, apply very high tension forces to these steel tendons using hydraulic jacks, then anchor them permanently at both ends of the span. As the stretched steel tries to shorten, it squeezes the surrounding concrete, creating strong compression forces in the lower fibres of the beam—exactly where traffic loads will later try to create tension.

When vehicles cross the bridge and cause it to bend, the bottom of the span must first overcome this pre-existing compression before any actual tension can develop. This pre-compression acts as a protective reserve, preventing the concrete from cracking under normal service loads. The entire cross-section remains intact and effective, allowing the span to carry much heavier loads than would otherwise be possible.

This works because we've reversed the stress state in the vulnerable zone before service loads arrive. The concrete never experiences net tension under normal traffic—it simply experiences reduced compression, staying within its strength capabilities.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text: The concrete bridge span is reinforced with high-strength steel cables or tendons that are tensioned before the concrete hardens. During construction, these steel elements are stretched by hydraulic jacks and anchored at the ends of the span. Once the concrete is poured and cured, the tension in the steel is released, transferring compressive force into the concrete. This compression pre-loads the entire span, especially the lower fibers that would normally be in tension under load. When traffic passes over the bridge, the bending forces first reduce this built-in compression rather than immediately stressing the concrete in tension. The steel cables absorb the tensile forces, while the concrete remains in compression, where it is strongest. This method prevents cracking under normal service loads and allows the span to support much heavier weights without damage. The steel and concrete work together as a composite system, each material doing what it does best: steel resisting tension and concrete resisting compression.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text:
To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic, high-strength steel cables or rods are embedded within the concrete. Before the bridge is put into service, these steel elements are stretched and anchored at the ends of the span. This stretching creates a powerful compressive force within the concrete, effectively "squeezing" it together. When traffic loads then try to bend the bridge and pull its underside apart, the pre-existing compressive force from the stretched steel counteracts this tension. This ensures that the concrete remains under compression, or at least experiences significantly reduced tension, preventing cracks from forming and allowing the bridge to safely carry much heavier loads.

This solution works because it proactively introduces a compressive stress into the concrete where tensile stresses would otherwise occur. Concrete is inherently strong in compression. By pre-compressing the concrete, any subsequent tensile forces from traffic loads must first overcome this built-in compression before the concrete itself experiences tension and begins to crack. This effectively shifts the stress state of the concrete into its strong compressive regime, even under bending.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can use a combination of steel reinforcement and pre-stressing techniques. Steel reinforcement bars, known as rebar, are embedded within the concrete, specifically in the lower portion of the span where tension occurs. These bars provide additional tensile strength to the concrete. Additionally, pre-stressing involves placing high-strength steel tendons within the concrete and applying tension to them before the bridge is subjected to service loads. The tendons are anchored at the ends of the span, maintaining tension and compressing the concrete. This compression counteracts the tensile forces that occur when the bridge is loaded, effectively increasing the load-bearing capacity and preventing cracking. By keeping the concrete in compression, even under heavy traffic loads, the bridge span can withstand large bending loads without compromising its structural integrity.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables running lengthwise through the concrete bridge span. Before the bridge opens to traffic, mechanically stretch these cables using hydraulic jacks, pulling them with tremendous force, then anchor the stretched cables firmly at both ends of the span. When released against the anchors, the cables try to contract but are held in place, squeezing the entire concrete span inward and downward, putting it under compression.

This pre-compression is carefully calculated so that when heavy traffic crosses the bridge and causes it to bend, the resulting tension forces in the bottom of the span merely reduce the compression rather than creating actual tension. The concrete never experiences the tensile stresses that would cause it to crack. Multiple individual cables allow precise control of the compression pattern, with more force applied where bending stresses will be greatest. The span can now carry its full theoretical load capacity because the concrete remains compressed throughout, working entirely within its natural strength as a compression material.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text: The solution is to build the bridge span using pre-stressed concrete. This is done by placing high-strength steel cables or rods inside the concrete formwork before the concrete is poured. Once the concrete has hardened and gained sufficient strength, these steel tendons are tensioned using powerful hydraulic jacks. This stretching of the steel puts the surrounding concrete into a state of compression, squeezing it tightly. When traffic loads are later applied to the bridge, causing the span to bend and the bottom to want to stretch, this initial compression must be relieved first. The traffic loads effectively work against the pre-applied squeeze. Because the concrete is very strong when being squeezed, it does not crack under the tension that would normally cause it to fail. The steel tendons, which are excellent at resisting stretching forces, carry the remaining tensile load. This combination allows the concrete beam to support much heavier loads without cracking, fully utilizing the material's natural strength in compression while the steel handles the tension.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text:
To prevent the concrete bridge from cracking under heavy traffic, high-strength steel cables are stretched tightly along the length of the bridge before the concrete is poured and allowed to harden. Once the concrete has set, the tension in these cables is carefully released. As the cables try to shorten, they pull the concrete inwards, effectively squeezing it together. This pre-squeezing puts the bottom of the bridge, which would normally be pulled apart by traffic, into a state of compression. When heavy vehicles drive over the bridge, the forces that would typically cause the concrete to crack are instead absorbed by this built-in compression, keeping the bridge intact and strong.

This works because the steel cables are segmented from the concrete's primary compressive role. The cables are specifically designed to carry the tensile forces, effectively pre-compressing the concrete where it would otherwise experience tension. This ensures the concrete remains in compression or at least neutral, preventing it from cracking under bending loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text: To address the issue of cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, the bridge can be designed with a segmented tension reinforcement system. This involves embedding a series of pre-tensioned steel cables or rods within the lower section of the concrete span. These steel elements are segmented into smaller, independent sections that can be individually tensioned and replaced if necessary. Each segment is anchored at both ends within the concrete, allowing it to bear tensile loads effectively. By pre-tensioning these steel segments before the concrete is poured, they compress the concrete, counteracting the tensile forces that occur when the bridge is under load. This segmentation allows for easy maintenance and replacement of individual segments without affecting the entire structure. Additionally, the use of high-strength, corrosion-resistant materials for the steel segments ensures durability and longevity. This approach enhances the overall tensile strength of the bridge span, preventing cracking and maintaining structural integrity under heavy traffic loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or tendons through ducts cast into the lower portion of the concrete bridge span. After the concrete has fully cured and gained strength, pull these tendons very tight using hydraulic jacks, stretching them significantly. Lock them in place at the span's ends using anchor plates and wedges. This tensioning process squeezes the concrete, putting the entire cross-section—especially the bottom fibers—into compression before any traffic crosses the bridge.

When vehicles load the bridge and it bends downward, the bottom would normally experience tension and crack. However, the bending forces must first relieve the pre-existing compression before any actual tension develops. Under normal service loads, the bottom concrete remains in compression, preventing cracks from forming. The steel tendons themselves carry any net tensile forces, since steel excels in tension. The concrete stays entirely in its strong compression zone, allowing the full cross-section to contribute to load-bearing capacity without cracking, dramatically increasing the span's effective strength and durability.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete on the underside of the bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic, high-strength steel cables or rods are embedded within the concrete. Before the concrete is poured, these steel tendons are stretched tightly by hydraulic jacks and held under extreme tension. The wet concrete is then cast around the stretched steel. Once the concrete has hardened and gained sufficient strength, the jacks are released. As the steel tendons try to contract back to their original length, they are prevented from doing so because they are now bonded to the solid concrete. This action forcibly compresses the entire concrete beam, squeezing it. When traffic loads later bend the bridge, the tension created on the bottom surface first has to cancel out this beneficial pre-applied compression. As a result, the concrete on the tension side remains in compression or experiences very low tension, effectively preventing cracks from forming. This allows the concrete to use its great strength in compression to support the load, while the embedded steel handles the tensile forces, making the entire structure much stronger.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic loads, high-strength steel bars are embedded within the concrete, particularly along the bottom of the span where it would otherwise experience stretching forces. These steel bars are incredibly strong when pulled, so they effectively carry the tension that would normally cause the concrete to crack. The concrete itself then primarily handles the squeezing forces, where it is very strong. This combination allows the bridge to withstand much larger bending loads without any cracking, ensuring its full strength is utilized and maintained over time.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, steel reinforcement bars, or rebar, are embedded within the concrete. These steel bars are placed in the lower section of the span where tensile forces are greatest. Steel is excellent at handling tension, unlike concrete, which is strong in compression but weak in tension. By using steel reinforcement, the tensile forces are effectively carried by the steel, while the concrete handles the compressive forces. This separation of functions allows the bridge span to withstand large bending loads without cracking. The combination of concrete and steel creates a reinforced concrete structure that is both strong and durable, ensuring the bridge can support heavy traffic loads safely and efficiently.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or tendons within the lower portion of the concrete bridge span before construction is complete. While the concrete is still setting or immediately after, stretch these steel tendons tight using hydraulic jacks and anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. This tensioning process squeezes the lower concrete fibers into compression.

When heavy traffic crosses the bridge and causes it to bend downward, the bottom of the span would normally stretch and crack. However, the pre-compressed concrete must first have its compression relieved before any actual tension can develop. The steel tendons, trying to contract back to their original length, continuously push inward on the concrete, keeping it compressed even under heavy loads.

The result is a bridge span where the concrete remains in compression throughout—the condition where it performs best. The lower portion becomes a specialized composite zone with properties tailored to resist the bending forces that occur there, while the upper portion remains standard concrete suited for handling compression from traffic weight.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text:
To prevent the underside of a concrete bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic loads, the concrete beam is built with high-strength steel cables or rods running through it, positioned near the bottom surface where tension occurs. Before the concrete hardens, these steel tendons are stretched tightly using hydraulic jacks, putting them under high tension. This stretching force compresses the entire concrete beam from end to end. Once the concrete sets, the jacks are released, and the steel tries to shrink back, but because it is bonded to the concrete, it keeps the concrete squeezed in compression.

When traffic moves across the bridge, the load tries to bend the span, stretching the bottom fibers. However, the built-in compression from the steel must be overcome first. The tensile stress from the traffic is carried by the steel, which is very strong in tension, while the concrete mainly experiences compression, which it handles well. This keeps the concrete from cracking under normal use. The steel and concrete work together as a team, with each material doing the job it is best at: steel resisting stretch, and concrete resisting crushing. The beam becomes much stronger for its size, lasting longer and supporting heavier loads safely.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic, we will build it with a special, tougher concrete layer along its bottom surface. This bottom layer, where the pulling forces are strongest, will be made from a high-performance concrete that has tiny fibers mixed into it. These fibers act like miniature internal reinforcement, making this specific part of the bridge much more resistant to stretching and cracking than regular concrete. The rest of the bridge span, which mostly experiences squeezing forces, will be made from standard, strong concrete. This way, the bridge is designed so that each part of its cross-section is made from the best material for the job it has to do, ensuring it can handle much heavier loads without developing cracks.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text:
To address the issue of concrete cracking under tension in a bridge span, we can enhance the structure by incorporating a composite design that uses different materials strategically. Specifically, we can embed steel reinforcement bars (rebar) within the lower portion of the concrete span. Steel is excellent in tension, complementing concrete's strength in compression. By placing the steel rebar primarily in the areas of the span that experience the most tension, we create a composite material that leverages the strengths of both components. Additionally, we can use pre-stressed concrete techniques, where steel tendons are tensioned before the concrete is poured. Once the concrete sets and the tension is released, the tendons compress the concrete, counteracting the tensile forces that occur when the bridge is in use. This approach ensures that the concrete remains under compression even when the bridge is loaded, preventing cracks from forming on the tension side.

This solution works because it transforms the bridge span into a composite structure where each material operates under conditions that maximize its strengths. The steel handles the tensile forces, while the concrete manages the compressive forces, resulting in a more durable and crack-resistant bridge span.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or strands in ducts running through the lower portion of the concrete span. Before the bridge opens to traffic, pull these cables to very high tension using hydraulic jacks, then anchor them permanently at the span ends. This process compresses the concrete throughout its depth, with the strongest compression in the bottom region where the cables are located.

When traffic loads cause the span to bend, the bottom surface would normally experience tension and crack. However, the pre-existing compression from the tensioned cables must first be overcome before any actual tension can develop. By carefully calculating the cable forces and positions, engineers ensure the bottom fibers remain in compression even under heavy traffic loads.

This works because the asymmetrically placed and tensioned cables create a beneficial stress pattern that directly counteracts the harmful tension caused by bending. The concrete stays compressed throughout, preventing cracks and allowing the full cross-section to contribute to carrying loads. The span achieves much higher effective strength without increasing its size.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text:
Design the concrete bridge span with an asymmetrical cross-section that is deeper and thicker at the bottom, where tension occurs under load, while the top remains relatively flat. This places more concrete and reinforcement precisely where it is needed to resist stretching and cracking. Additionally, embed high-strength steel tendons near the lower surface, tensioned before the bridge is loaded, to squeeze the concrete from below. This pre-compression counteracts the tensile forces from traffic, so the concrete never experiences net tension and thus does not crack. The result is a span that fully uses concrete’s natural compressive strength, handles heavy bending loads safely, and avoids the weakness of untreated tension.

This solution works because it matches the shape and internal forces to the actual demands of bending: extra material and pre-stressing on the tension side neutralize stretching forces, while the compression side remains robust. By focusing strength where it is most needed, the bridge performs efficiently without cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text: The bridge span will be designed with a distinctively asymmetrical cross-section. Its underside will feature a significantly deeper and wider flange compared to its top surface, creating a profile that resembles a stout "T" or an inverted "L" rather than a symmetrical rectangle or "I" shape. Within this enlarged lower section, a dense network of high-strength steel reinforcing bars will be embedded, concentrated closer to the bottom surface. This strategic placement of both concrete mass and steel reinforcement directly counteracts the tensile forces that cause cracking. The span's depth will also vary along its length, being deepest at the center where bending stresses are highest, and gradually tapering towards the supports. This design ensures that the material is precisely where it's needed most to resist the pulling forces, preventing the concrete from cracking under heavy traffic loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text:
To address the issue of cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can redesign the cross-section of the span to be asymmetrical. Instead of a uniform rectangular cross-section, the span can be modified to have a trapezoidal shape with a wider base on the tension side. This wider base can accommodate additional reinforcement, such as steel rebar or tension cables, which are specifically designed to handle tensile forces. By increasing the amount of reinforcement on the tension side, the span can better resist the tensile stresses that cause cracking. Additionally, the asymmetrical shape can be further optimized by incorporating a slight curvature or camber in the span, which naturally distributes the loads more evenly and reduces the tensile stress concentration on the underside. This design not only enhances the structural integrity of the bridge span but also ensures that it can withstand heavy traffic loads without compromising its durability.

This solution works because it directly addresses the weakness of concrete in tension by increasing the reinforcement where it is most needed. The asymmetrical design allows for a more efficient distribution of materials, ensuring that the span can handle larger bending loads without cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or strands through hollow ducts running lengthwise through the concrete bridge span. Before the bridge opens to traffic, pull these steel tendons to extremely high tension forces using hydraulic jacks, then anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. This process squeezes the entire concrete cross-section into compression, including the bottom surface that will later face tension when vehicles cross.

When heavy traffic loads the bridge and it bends downward, the bottom surface tries to stretch and crack. However, the tension forces from bending must first cancel out the pre-existing compression before any actual tension develops in the concrete. By setting the initial compression high enough, the concrete bottom remains compressed (or neutral) even under maximum traffic loads, preventing cracks entirely.

This approach allows the full cross-section to contribute to load-bearing capacity. The steel provides the tension resistance it excels at, while the concrete works entirely in compression where it's strongest, creating a bridge span that efficiently resists heavy bending loads without cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text: The solution is to build the bridge span not from plain concrete, but as a composite structure by embedding high-strength steel bars, known as reinforcement, inside the concrete. These steel bars are placed specifically along the bottom part of the span, which experiences the most tension when the bridge bends under traffic. The concrete is poured around this steel skeleton, bonding the two materials together permanently. When a load is applied, the concrete naturally tries to resist the squeezing forces on its top side, which it does very well. Meanwhile, the stretching forces on the bottom are carried by the embedded steel bars, which are extremely strong in tension. Because the steel takes on the tensile stress, the concrete on the underside is prevented from cracking. This allows the entire cross-section of the bridge to contribute to its strength, resulting in a much stronger and more durable span that can safely carry heavy traffic loads over time. The system works because the concrete and steel work together as a single unit, each material doing the job it is best suited for.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the concrete bridge span, high-strength steel cables are embedded within the concrete along its length. These cables are then stretched tightly and anchored securely at both ends of the span before the bridge is put into service. This stretching action causes the cables to pull inward, which in turn squeezes the surrounding concrete, putting it under constant compression. When heavy traffic loads cause the bridge to bend and try to pull the underside of the concrete apart, this pre-existing squeezing force from the cables resists the pulling. The concrete effectively remains under compression, or at least experiences very little tension, preventing it from cracking and allowing the bridge to safely carry much heavier loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text:
To address the issue of cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can merge the concrete with steel reinforcement to create a composite structure known as reinforced concrete. Steel is strong in tension, complementing the concrete's strength in compression. By embedding steel rebar within the lower portion of the concrete span, we effectively combine the two materials to work together under load. The steel bars are placed in the areas where tension occurs, allowing them to absorb the tensile forces that would otherwise cause the concrete to crack. This merging of materials ensures that the concrete remains in compression, while the steel handles the tension, significantly enhancing the overall load-bearing capacity of the bridge span.

This solution works because it leverages the strengths of both materials: concrete's compressive strength and steel's tensile strength. By integrating them into a single system, the bridge span can withstand larger bending loads without cracking, maintaining structural integrity and safety under heavy traffic conditions. This approach is widely used in civil engineering to ensure the durability and longevity of concrete structures.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or strands through hollow ducts running lengthwise through the concrete bridge span. Before the bridge opens to traffic, use hydraulic jacks to stretch these steel tendons to extremely high tension forces, then anchor them permanently at both ends of the span. This process squeezes the entire concrete cross-section into compression, especially the bottom portion that will later face tension when vehicles cross above.

When heavy traffic loads cause the span to bend downward, the bottom fibers try to stretch and crack. However, the pre-applied compression from the tensioned steel must first be overcome before any actual tension develops in the concrete. The steel cables serve double duty: they reinforce the structure while simultaneously generating beneficial compressive forces that counteract traffic-induced tension. Under normal loads, the bottom of the span remains compressed or stress-neutral rather than entering tension, preventing cracks from forming. The span can now safely carry much heavier loads because the concrete works entirely within its compressive strength range, where it performs best.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking under tension while maintaining its ability to carry heavy traffic loads, the concrete itself is enhanced with tiny, high-strength fibers mixed evenly throughout the entire structure. These fibers, which can be made of steel or a durable synthetic material, are uniformly dispersed in the concrete when it is poured. As the bridge span bends under load, the concrete on the underside experiences tension, but instead of cracking, the countless fibers embedded within it work together to resist the stretching forces. This turns the entire concrete volume into a composite material that handles both compression and tension effectively. The fibers act like a built-in reinforcement network, making the concrete stronger and more flexible without needing separate steel bars placed only in certain areas. This approach ensures that every part of the bridge contributes to resisting bending stresses, so the structure remains intact and performs reliably under service conditions, utilizing the full potential of its cross-section without cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text:
To prevent cracking and enhance the load-bearing capacity of the concrete bridge span, high-strength steel cables or bars are embedded within the concrete during its construction. Once the concrete has hardened, these internal steel elements are pulled taut and anchored at the ends of the span. This process, called post-tensioning, introduces a compressive force into the concrete, particularly in the lower sections that would otherwise experience tension under traffic loads. By pre-compressing the concrete, the steel elements effectively counteract the tensile stresses caused by bending, preventing the concrete from cracking. This integrated system allows the concrete to remain in compression or experience very low tension, fully utilizing its inherent strength and significantly increasing the bridge's ability to withstand heavy loads without damage.

This solution works because the steel tendons perform a dual function: they provide the necessary tensile strength that concrete lacks, and by being tensioned, they actively pre-compress the concrete, effectively eliminating the tensile stresses that would otherwise cause cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text:
To address the issue of concrete cracking under tension in a bridge span, we can integrate a universal reinforcement system that combines multiple functions into a single solution. This involves embedding a composite material, such as a steel-reinforced polymer, within the concrete span. The composite material is designed to handle tensile forces effectively, thus preventing cracking. The steel component provides the necessary tensile strength, while the polymer acts as a binding agent, ensuring flexibility and durability. This reinforcement system is standardized and can be used across various bridge designs, eliminating the need for separate tension-handling mechanisms.

Additionally, the composite material can be pre-stressed before the concrete is poured. This pre-stressing process involves stretching the steel reinforcement, which compresses the concrete when released, counteracting the tensile forces experienced during heavy traffic loads. By using this universal reinforcement system, the bridge span can withstand large bending loads without cracking, maintaining its structural integrity and extending its lifespan. This solution is efficient, cost-effective, and adaptable to different bridge designs, ensuring that the concrete remains strong and durable under tension.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P07 Nesting
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables inside hollow ducts that run lengthwise through the concrete bridge span during construction. After the concrete hardens but before the bridge opens to traffic, pull these cables to extremely high tension forces and anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. This process squeezes the entire concrete cross-section into compression, especially the bottom portion that would normally crack under traffic loads.

The result is that under normal service loads, the bottom of the span remains in compression or experiences only minimal tension—far below the cracking threshold. This keeps the concrete intact and allows the full cross-section to resist loads effectively, dramatically increasing the span's practical load-carrying capacity without cracking.

This works because the internal cables create a reservoir of compressive force that neutralizes the tension caused by bending, keeping the concrete in its strong compression state throughout the span's depth.

outputs.text:

As the steel tendons try to shrink back to their original length, they pull inward on the ends of the concrete beam, squeezing the entire span and putting the bottom part into a state of compression.

When traffic later drives over the bridge, the weight tries to bend the span downwards, which would normally pull the bottom apart and cause tension cracks. The two forces oppose each other, so the net effect is that the concrete at the bottom experiences much less tension, or may even stay in compression. Since concrete is very strong when squeezed but weak when pulled, this pre-stressing technique allows the bridge to carry much heavier loads without cracking. outputs.label: TRIZ on + P15 Dynamization

Conversely, when the load decreases, the tension in the tendons is reduced to maintain optimal structural integrity.

This solution works because it dynamically adjusts the internal forces within the bridge span, ensuring that the concrete remains in compression even under varying loads. By actively managing the tension forces, the system prevents cracking and enhances the overall durability and safety of the bridge.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P16 Partial or Excessive Actions
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or rods along the bottom portion of the concrete bridge span before it enters service. These cables are stretched tight and anchored firmly at both ends of the span, creating significant tension in the steel itself. This tension pulls the concrete together, squeezing it and creating strong compressive forces throughout the entire cross-section, especially in the lower regions.

When the bridge is initially pre-stressed this way, it actually curves slightly upward. Once traffic loads are applied, the bridge bends downward under the weight, but this bending works against the pre-existing compression rather than creating new tension. The lower fibers, which would normally crack under tension, instead experience a reduction in their compressive stress but remain in compression overall.

The key is applying more initial compression than strictly necessary, so even under the heaviest expected traffic loads, the bottom of the span never transitions from compression into tension. This prevents cracks from forming and allows the concrete to utilize its full compressive strength, dramatically increasing the load-carrying capacity of the span.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P16 Partial or Excessive Actions
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking on its underside under heavy traffic loads, high-strength steel cables or rods are strategically placed inside the concrete where tension occurs. Before the bridge is opened to traffic, these steel elements are stretched by hydraulic jacks, putting them under very high tension. This stretching force is transferred to the concrete, squeezing it and putting the entire cross-section, especially the lower fibers, into a state of compression. This initial compression is designed to be stronger than the tensile forces that will be created by the weight of the traffic. When vehicles then drive across the bridge, their weight tries to bend the span and create tension on the bottom, but this tension first has to overcome the built-in compression. Because the pre-applied compression is greater, the concrete never actually goes into tension and therefore does not crack. The steel cables, now permanently locked in place, continuously maintain this beneficial compression throughout the life of the bridge. This allows the concrete, which is naturally very strong in compression, to carry the load effectively without suffering from its weakness in tension. The bridge becomes much stronger and more durable, capable of supporting the required heavy loads safely.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P16 Partial or Excessive Actions
outputs.text: To ensure the bridge span can withstand heavy traffic without cracking, high-strength steel cables are threaded through ducts within the concrete. Once the concrete has hardened, these cables are stretched very tightly using powerful hydraulic jacks and then anchored securely at the ends of the span. This stretching action pulls the concrete together, putting it under a strong squeezing force, particularly along its bottom surface where it would normally be pulled apart by traffic. When vehicles drive over the bridge, the weight tries to bend the span and pull the bottom surface into tension. However, because the concrete is already being squeezed so tightly by the stretched cables, the traffic loads are not strong enough to overcome this initial squeezing force. As a result, the concrete on the underside of the bridge remains compressed, or at least experiences very little pulling, preventing any cracks from forming and allowing the bridge to carry much heavier loads safely.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P16 Partial or Excessive Actions
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can use a technique called prestressing. This involves embedding steel cables within the concrete and tensioning them before the bridge is subjected to traffic loads. The cables are stretched and anchored at the ends of the span, creating a compressive force in the concrete. This pre-compression counteracts the tensile forces that occur when the bridge is loaded, effectively preventing cracks. By applying slightly more compression than necessary, we ensure that even under maximum load, the concrete remains in compression, thus avoiding cracks. This method allows the bridge to withstand large bending loads without compromising its structural integrity, ensuring safety and longevity.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P17 Transition to Another Dimension
outputs.text:
Install high-strength steel cables or tendons through ducts that run lengthwise through the concrete span, positioned near the bottom surface. Before the bridge opens to traffic, use hydraulic jacks to stretch these cables to very high tension forces, then anchor them firmly at both ends of the span. This process is called prestressing.

When the cables are tensioned, they try to shorten, which squeezes the concrete along its entire length and creates a strong compressive force throughout the beam, especially at the bottom. Now when heavy traffic crosses the bridge and causes it to bend downward, the bending action tries to stretch the bottom fibers. However, instead of going directly into tension and cracking, the bottom concrete must first overcome the compressive force already locked into it by the prestressed cables. 

The result is that under normal traffic loads, the bottom of the span remains in compression or reaches only neutral stress, never actual tension. Since the concrete never experiences tension, it cannot crack, allowing the full cross-section to contribute to carrying the load effectively.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P17 Transition to Another Dimension
outputs.text: The solution is to embed high-strength steel cables or rods inside the concrete bridge span before it is fully constructed. These steel elements are stretched tightly by hydraulic jacks while the concrete is still wet and flexible. After the concrete hardens and gains strength, the tension on the steel is slowly released. This causes the steel to want to return to its original length, but because it is now bonded to the concrete, it instead squeezes the entire span, putting the concrete into a state of compression. Specifically, the underside of the bridge, which would normally be in tension under traffic loads, is now pre-compressed. When heavy vehicles pass over, the bending forces first have to overcome this built-in compression before they can put the concrete into tension. This prevents cracking because the concrete remains in compression or experiences much lower tension. The steel handles any remaining tensile forces, making the span much stronger and more durable under heavy loads. This works because it uses steel's tensile strength to lock beneficial compression into the concrete, ensuring the material stays in its strong compressive state even during bending.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P17 Transition to Another Dimension
outputs.text: To prevent cracking on the underside of the concrete bridge span, we will embed a sophisticated network of high-strength steel cables within the concrete, particularly in the lower section. These cables will not be laid in a simple flat grid, but rather in multiple, slightly offset layers, some even gently curving or weaving through the concrete. Before the bridge is fully loaded, these steel cables will be stretched and anchored, effectively squeezing the surrounding concrete. This internal squeezing action puts the concrete under constant compression. When heavy traffic then tries to bend the bridge and pull its underside apart, the pre-stressed steel cables, arranged in their multi-layered pattern, immediately resist this pulling force. This ensures the concrete itself remains compressed or experiences only very minimal tension, preventing any cracks from forming and allowing the bridge to safely carry much heavier loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P17 Transition to Another Dimension
outputs.text:
To enhance the load-bearing capacity of the concrete bridge span and prevent cracking on its tension side, we can transition from a traditional single-layer design to a multi-layered approach. By incorporating a composite structure, we can add a layer of high-strength steel reinforcement or fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) on the underside of the bridge span. This additional layer will act as a tensile reinforcement, effectively absorbing the tensile stresses that occur when the bridge is under heavy traffic loads. The steel or FRP layer can be bonded to the concrete using advanced adhesives or mechanical fasteners, ensuring a strong connection between the materials.

Furthermore, the bridge span can be reoriented by slightly arching it upwards, creating a cambered profile. This arching will help distribute the loads more evenly across the span, reducing the tensile stresses on the underside. By combining these strategies, the bridge span can better withstand large bending loads without the concrete cracking, thus maintaining its structural integrity and extending its service life. This solution leverages the strengths of both materials and optimizes the bridge's performance under heavy traffic conditions.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P18 Mechanical Vibration
outputs.text:
Install an array of small vibrating devices embedded in the concrete along the bottom surface of the bridge span. These devices use piezoelectric elements (crystals that vibrate when electricity is applied) to generate ultrasonic vibrations—oscillations at frequencies far above what humans can hear, typically 20,000 to 40,000 cycles per second.

When the bridge carries heavy traffic and begins to bend, strain sensors detect the loading and activate the vibrating elements. The ultrasonic vibrations create rapid, alternating waves of compression that travel through the concrete. These high-frequency oscillations counteract the steady pulling forces that would normally cause cracks to form and grow on the bottom of the span.

The vibration works by constantly interrupting the tensile stress pattern—the concrete experiences such rapid alternating forces that cracks cannot establish themselves. The system operates only when needed, powered by a low-voltage electrical supply connected to the embedded sensors and vibrators. This allows the bridge span to utilize its full cross-sectional strength without cracking under service loads.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P18 Mechanical Vibration
outputs.text:
To prevent the concrete on the underside of the bridge span from cracking under heavy traffic loads, a network of small, flat ceramic discs is embedded within the concrete during construction, specifically placed near the bottom surface where tension occurs. These discs are piezoelectric actuators, which are materials that change shape very slightly when an electric voltage is applied to them. The bridge is equipped with sensors that constantly measure the strain, detecting when the bridge begins to bend under a load. When the sensors signal that tension is building, a control system sends a rapidly alternating electrical signal to the embedded discs. This causes the discs to vibrate or oscillate extremely quickly, millions of times per second, at their optimal resonant frequency.

This high-speed vibration transmits energy through the surrounding concrete. The effect is to create a persistent, shimmering state of microscopic compression right where the concrete wants to stretch and crack. It’s like constantly jiggling the material to keep it from settling into a state where cracks can start and grow. The system works automatically and only when needed, counteracting the destructive tensile forces with beneficial compressive forces generated from within the material itself. This allows the concrete to withstand much greater bending loads without damage, fully utilizing its natural strength in compression to carry the weight.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P18 Mechanical Vibration
outputs.text: To prevent the concrete bridge span from cracking on its underside, a network of small, electrically activated ceramic devices will be embedded within the concrete along the bottom surface. These devices will be designed to vibrate at a very high frequency, similar to ultrasound, when an electrical current is applied. This high-frequency vibration will be continuously active or triggered when heavy traffic is present. The vibrations will be carefully tuned to a specific frequency that resonates with the concrete material itself. This constant, microscopic jiggling will prevent tiny cracks from forming and growing, effectively strengthening the concrete's ability to resist stretching forces. This ensures the bridge can handle heavy loads without its structural integrity being compromised by cracking.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P18 Mechanical Vibration
outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can embed piezoelectric materials within the concrete. These materials will generate controlled vibrations at specific frequencies, particularly the resonant frequency of the structure. Additionally, during the curing process, these vibrations compact the concrete, resulting in a denser and more uniform material. This approach not only minimizes the likelihood of cracking but also enhances the overall durability and load-bearing capacity of the bridge span. By integrating electromagnetic fields with the vibrations, we can further optimize stress distribution, ensuring the bridge can withstand large bending loads without compromising its structural integrity.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P19 Periodic Action
outputs.text:
Install hydraulic actuators at strategic points along the underside of the bridge span. These actuators apply periodic upward lifting forces on a controlled cycle—activating for several seconds every few minutes. When activated, they temporarily push the span upward, creating a slight camber that compresses the lower concrete fibers rather than stretching them. This periodic compression counteracts the pulling forces from traffic loads above.

Between lifting pulses, sensors continuously monitor the bridge's strain and detect any crack formation. The control system uses this data to adjust how often the actuators activate, how long they push, and how hard, responding to real-time traffic conditions. During light traffic periods, the actuators pulse less frequently; during rush hours with heavy vehicles, they activate more often.

This creates a dynamic balance where the concrete alternates between compression from the actuators and tension from traffic, but never experiences the sustained pulling forces that cause progressive cracking. The result is a bridge span that maintains its full structural capacity by keeping the concrete working primarily in compression, its natural strength.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P19 Periodic Action
outputs.text: Embed smart actuators made of materials like piezoelectric ceramics or shape-memory alloys into the lower part of the concrete bridge span. These actuators are connected to sensors that monitor stress in real time. When the sensors detect that the concrete is about to crack due to tension from bending loads, the actuators quickly contract in brief, repeated pulses. This contraction applies a compressive force directly to the tension zone, counteracting the stretching and preventing cracks from forming. The pulses are short and frequent, matching the rhythm of the traffic loads, and they stop when the tension subsides. Between pulses, the system rests and prepares for the next action, conserving energy and allowing for continuous adjustment based on incoming sensor data. This approach keeps the concrete in compression where it is weak in tension, enabling the span to handle much larger bending forces without damage. The bridge remains strong and durable, with the embedded system working invisibly to maintain integrity under heavy use.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P19 Periodic Action

When heavy vehicles pass over the bridge, these internal components would periodically expand or contract, creating a brief but strong squeezing force on the concrete where it would otherwise be pulled apart. This intermittent squeezing action would counteract the pulling forces from the traffic, preventing the concrete from stretching too much and developing cracks. The timing and strength of these internal squeezes would be adjusted automatically based on the weight and speed of the traffic, ensuring the concrete remains intact and strong under all conditions.

outputs.text: To prevent cracking in the tension side of a concrete bridge span, we can use a system of pre-stressed tendons that are periodically tensioned and released. The key innovation is to periodically adjust the tension in these tendons using hydraulic jacks. By increasing or decreasing the tension at regular intervals, the concrete experiences varying compressive forces, which helps redistribute stresses and prevent cracks. During the intervals when the tendons are not being adjusted, maintenance checks or structural health monitoring can be performed. This approach enhances the bridge's ability to withstand large bending loads without cracking, ensuring its structural integrity and longevity.

Under normal service loads, the bottom of the span never reaches tension—it simply becomes less compressed. outputs.text:

When traffic loads later cause the bridge to bend, the bending force first has to overcome this built-in compression before it can put any tension into the concrete. This approach keeps the concrete continuously compressed throughout its depth, adapting in real-time to changing loads. Since concrete never experiences tension, it cannot crack, allowing the full cross-section to contribute to carrying the load.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P23 Feedback
outputs.text: The solution is to build an active system directly into the concrete bridge span that senses when the bottom is starting to stretch and immediately pushes back to stop it from cracking.
