It only switches on the power to the conductive grid when the conditions are just right for ice to form—specifically, when the temperature is near or below freezing and the air is moist. When activated, a safe, low-level electrical current flows through the grid, generating just enough heat to warm the surface of the sleeve slightly above freezing. This gentle heat prevents any water from turning into ice. As soon as the weather warms up or the precipitation stops, the sensor turns the heating grid off completely. This means the system uses energy only during the specific times when ice is a real threat, avoiding any waste. The sleeve itself is tough, lightweight, and does not interfere with the flow of electricity through the main cable inside.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P30 Flexible Shells and Thin Films
outputs.text: To prevent ice from accumulating on overhead power lines, a very thin, flexible, and transparent film is permanently bonded to the outside of each line. This film contains tiny electrical pathways and miniature sensors that can detect when ice is starting to form. When the sensors detect cold temperatures and moisture, indicating ice is likely, a small amount of electricity is sent through the film in the affected areas. This generates a gentle warmth, just enough to melt the ice before it can build up and become heavy. The film is designed to be extremely light and durable, adding almost no weight to the lines. This system only activates when needed, ensuring that electricity isn't wasted heating the lines when there's no risk of ice.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P30 Flexible Shells and Thin Films
outputs.text: The solution involves wrapping overhead power-transmission lines with a flexible, transparent polymer film that acts as a protective barrier against ice formation. This film is hydrophobic, causing water to bead and roll off, preventing ice buildup. It also includes a thin conductive layer that can be selectively heated. Sensors monitor the temperature and activate the heating only when conditions are right for ice formation, conserving energy when it's not needed. The film's flexibility allows it to adapt to the lines' shape and temperature changes, ensuring continuous protection without disrupting electricity flow. This approach effectively prevents ice damage while minimizing energy use, maintaining the integrity and functionality of the power lines.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P31 Porous Materials
outputs.text:
Install lightweight, porous polymer sleeves around critical sections of overhead power lines. These sleeves contain interconnected air pockets filled with a special phase-change material that melts at temperatures slightly above freezing (around 5-10°C).

During warm weather, the material inside remains liquid and the sleeve has minimal effect on operations. When temperatures drop and ice threatens to form, operators briefly increase the electrical current flowing through the line. This generates heat through normal electrical resistance in the conductor. This heat melts the phase-change material trapped within the porous sleeve structure.

As the material melts, it absorbs significant thermal energy. Over the following hours, this stored energy is slowly released, keeping the outer surface of the power line just above freezing temperature. This prevents ice from forming or adhering to the line. The porous structure ensures the heat distributes evenly around the entire circumference of the conductor.

This approach uses only short bursts of heating to "charge" the thermal storage system, rather than continuous heating, making it far more energy-efficient than conventional de-icing methods.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P31 Porous Materials
outputs.text: A practical solution is to cover the overhead power lines with a special porous coating that contains a hidden heat-releasing substance. This coating is designed to be highly water-repellent, so water beads up and does not easily freeze on the surface. Inside the tiny pores of the coating, there is a material that changes from liquid to solid when temperatures drop, and this process gives off a small amount of heat. When ice starts to form, the water seeps into the pores and causes this material to freeze, releasing just enough warmth to melt the ice immediately around it. Once the weather warms up above freezing, the material thaws again and reabsorbs heat from the air, getting ready for the next cold spell. This system works automatically, using natural temperature changes to prevent ice buildup without any external power, and it does nothing when the weather is warm, so no energy is wasted. The lines stay clear, electricity flows uninterrupted, and the system maintains itself through seasonal cycles.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P31 Porous Materials
outputs.text: Power lines can be protected from ice buildup by applying a special coating. This coating is made from a flexible material that contains many tiny, hollow spaces, like a sponge. These tiny spaces are filled with a substance that can store and release heat. When the air temperature drops to around freezing, this substance inside the coating releases a small amount of stored warmth. This gentle heat prevents ice from sticking firmly to the power line. Instead, any ice that tries to form will be loose and easily blown off by the wind or shaken off by the natural vibrations of the line. When the weather warms up, the substance in the coating absorbs heat from the air, recharging itself for the next cold spell. This way, the lines stay clear of ice without needing to be actively heated, saving energy and ensuring a continuous power supply.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P31 Porous Materials
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, a specialized coating can be applied to the lines. This coating is made from a lightweight, durable composite material with embedded micro-pores. These pores are filled with a phase-change material (PCM) that melts at a temperature slightly above freezing. When the temperature drops and ice begins to form, the PCM absorbs heat from the environment as it changes from solid to liquid, warming the surface of the power line and preventing ice accumulation. As temperatures rise above freezing, the PCM solidifies, ready to absorb heat during the next cold spell. This system does not require external energy input, as it relies on the natural phase-change properties of the material. The solution is self-regulating and energy-efficient, activating only when necessary and without the need for external power sources.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P32 Color Changes
outputs.text:
Apply special temperature-sensitive coatings to power transmission lines at regular intervals. These coatings change color when temperatures drop to freezing levels where ice can form. Automated cameras or drones patrol the lines and detect these color changes, triggering a control system that activates electrical heating only in the affected line segments.

Additionally, apply fluorescent or light-emitting markers along the lines that glow when ice accumulates on them. Sensors using ultraviolet or infrared light can detect this glow even in darkness or poor weather, providing real-time information about where ice is building up and how thick it has become.

This detection system ensures that heating activates only when temperatures are at freezing and ice is actually present on the lines. The electricity continues flowing normally to customers while targeted heating removes ice from problem areas. During warmer weather, the coatings don't change color, so no heating occurs and no energy is wasted. The visual monitoring system provides continuous surveillance without requiring manual inspections of remote transmission lines.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P32 Color Changes
outputs.text: The power lines are coated with a special material that glows brightly under ultraviolet light when the temperature drops below freezing. This glow makes any ice formation highly visible and serves as a signal for an automated heating system. When sensors detect this color change along with actual ice buildup, they activate a targeted heating mechanism, such as resistive elements within the lines or external induction heaters. This heating melts the ice immediately, preventing damage. The system only turns on during freezing conditions, ensuring that no energy is wasted when the weather is warm and ice cannot form. Electricity continues to flow normally throughout the process, with no interruption to supply. The ultraviolet light required for the glow is provided by low-energy, weatherproof lamps mounted alongside the lines, designed to operate efficiently and reliably.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P32 Color Changes
outputs.text: Power lines can be coated with a special material that changes color when the temperature drops to near freezing. Normally, the lines would appear dark. However, as the air temperature approaches freezing, this coating would visibly shift to a lighter color, like white or light blue. Cameras or sensors positioned along the power line route would continuously monitor for this color change. When the lighter color is detected, it signals that conditions are right for ice to form. At that point, a heating system built into the power line would automatically turn on, gently warming the line just enough to melt any accumulating ice. Once the ice is gone and the line's surface warms above freezing, the coating would revert to its original dark color, and the heating system would switch off. This ensures ice is removed only when needed, saving energy.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P32 Color Changes
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, a special coating can be applied to the lines that changes color based on temperature and moisture levels. This coating will turn a specific color when the temperature approaches freezing and moisture is present, indicating that ice formation is likely. This color change can automatically trigger a heating system that activates only when necessary, preventing ice from forming without wasting energy during warmer conditions. The coating can also include luminescent properties, making it visible under certain lighting conditions, which allows for easy monitoring from a distance, even at night or during bad weather. This solution ensures that de-icing measures are only used when needed, reducing energy consumption and preventing damage to the power lines, while maintaining a continuous electricity supply.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P33 Homogeneity
outputs.text: Install a water misting system along the power transmission lines that activates automatically when ice begins to form. The system sprays a fine mist of water onto the conductors when sensors detect freezing conditions and ice accumulation. The sprayed water creates a thin liquid layer between the metal conductor and the accumulated ice, which dramatically weakens the bond holding the ice to the line. This interfacial layer prevents strong adhesion from forming. The latent heat released as the misted water freezes also provides a slight warming effect that further loosens existing ice. Once the bond is weakened, the ice slides off the lines under its own weight or with help from wind, falling safely to the ground below. Temperature and ice sensors ensure the system operates only during actual icing conditions, so no energy is wasted heating lines during warmer weather when ice cannot form. The water can be sourced from collected rainwater or snow melt, making the system sustainable and inexpensive to operate while maintaining continuous electrical service.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P33 Homogeneity
outputs.text:
The solution involves modifying the power line by adding a thin outer layer made of a material very similar to the line's core conductor. This special layer is bonded directly to the main cable. Sensors are installed along the line to continuously monitor for the specific conditions that lead to ice formation, such as temperature and humidity. When ice begins to form, a control system activates a secondary electrical circuit. This circuit sends a brief, high-frequency electrical signal along the power line, superimposed on the normal electricity flow.

This high-frequency signal does not significantly heat the entire cable. Instead, it causes the specially designed outer layer to vibrate or undergo tiny, rapid expansions and contractions. Because this outer layer is firmly attached to the main conductor but has a different response to the electrical signal than the ice, the bond between the ice and the line is mechanically broken. The ice cracks and falls off under its own weight. The key is that this de-icing signal is only sent when the sensors detect actual ice buildup. During normal, ice-free conditions, the system remains off, so no energy is wasted. The power supply continues uninterrupted throughout the entire process, as the de-icing signal is compatible with the standard electrical transmission.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P33 Homogeneity
outputs.text: Power transmission lines can be designed with an outer coating that mimics ice in its ability to form and release. This coating would be a specialized polymer or composite material engineered to have extremely low adhesion to ice, and to itself, when a small amount of heat is applied. When freezing conditions are detected, this outer layer would be subtly warmed by the current flowing through the line, just enough to create a weak, sacrificial interface. Any ice that forms would then bond to this easily releasable layer. As the ice accumulates, its weight, combined with the minimal heat from the line, would cause the entire ice sheath to detach and fall away cleanly, without damaging the conductor or requiring significant energy expenditure. This process would be automatically controlled by sensors.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P33 Homogeneity
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, the lines can be coated with a hydrophobic polymer that has thermal properties similar to ice. This coating repels water, preventing ice from forming initially. If ice does form, the similar thermal expansion properties between the coating and ice ensure that the ice does not adhere strongly to the lines. This makes it easier for natural forces, such as wind or vibrations from the lines, to dislodge the ice without requiring additional energy. This approach minimizes the risk of damage to the lines and eliminates the need for energy-intensive de-icing methods, ensuring continuous electricity supply without interruption.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P34 Discarding and Recovering
outputs.text:
Install ice detection sensors along power transmission lines connected to a control system. When sensors detect ice formation, the system briefly increases electrical current through the lines for a short pulse (typically 30-60 seconds). This controlled heating raises the line temperature just enough to melt the thin interface layer between the metal conductor and the accumulated ice. Once this bond layer melts, the ice sheet loses adhesion and falls away under its own weight. The system then immediately returns the current to normal transmission levels.

The sensors continuously monitor temperature and humidity conditions, activating the de-icing pulse only when ice is actually present. During above-freezing weather, the system remains inactive, consuming no extra energy. The brief heating pulses use the existing electrical infrastructure, requiring no external heating elements or interruption to power delivery. Modern sensors and automated controls make this process responsive and efficient, targeting only the line segments where ice has formed rather than heating the entire network unnecessarily.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P34 Discarding and Recovering
outputs.text: The solution is to coat each power line with a thin, durable outer sleeve that contains a network of fine, embedded heating wires. This sleeve is normally inactive and adds almost no energy cost. When sensors detect the start of ice accumulation, a control system sends a short, low-power electrical pulse through the heating wires within that specific section of line. This pulse slightly warms the surface of the sleeve, just enough to melt the microscopic bond where the ice touches the coating. Once this bond is broken, the ice sheet loses its grip and slides off under its own weight, clearing the line. The sleeve is tough enough to last for years, but if it ever wears out, maintenance crews can apply a new coating during scheduled outages. This approach removes ice only when it actually forms, avoids heating the lines in above-freezing weather, and keeps electricity flowing continuously because the brief pulse does not interfere with the main power transmission.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P34 Discarding and Recovering
outputs.text: Power lines can be designed with a special outer layer that contains tiny, embedded heating wires. These wires are very thin and are only meant to be used once. When sensors detect that ice is building up on the line, a quick burst of electricity is sent through these tiny wires. This burst makes the wires heat up extremely fast, instantly melting the ice that is stuck to the line. As the ice melts and falls off, the tiny wires themselves vaporize or break down, disappearing from the line. This means no energy is wasted heating the line when there's no ice. If ice builds up again later, a new section of the line with fresh embedded wires can be installed, or the line could be designed with segments that can be easily replaced.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P34 Discarding and Recovering
outputs.text: The solution involves applying a special conductive coating to overhead power-transmission lines, which can generate heat when needed. This coating is activated by a network of smart sensors that monitor environmental conditions like temperature and humidity. When these sensors detect conditions conducive to ice formation, they trigger the conductive coating to heat up, melting any ice that has formed. This system operates only when necessary, conserving energy by remaining inactive when the weather is above freezing and no ice can form. The sensors are powered by small solar panels, allowing them to function independently without drawing power from the grid. This approach ensures that ice is removed efficiently without interrupting the electricity supply or wasting energy.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P35 Parameter Changes
outputs.text:
Install an automated ice-removal system that detects ice buildup on power lines using sensors that monitor line weight, vibration, and weather conditions. When ice forms, the system temporarily increases the electrical current flowing through the transmission lines themselves. The conductors naturally generate heat due to electrical resistance, and this controlled boost in current raises their temperature enough to melt the thin layer of ice directly touching the metal surface. Once this interface melts, the ice shell loses its grip and falls away from the line under its own weight.

The system includes smart controls that activate heating only when sensors confirm actual ice formation - specifically when temperatures drop below freezing and moisture is present. During warmer weather or dry conditions, the system remains inactive, avoiding energy waste. The entire process maintains continuous electricity transmission to customers since it uses the same lines that carry power. The heating cycle typically lasts only minutes, just long enough to release the ice, then automatically returns the lines to normal operating conditions.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P35 Parameter Changes
outputs.text:
A system is installed on the power lines that prevents ice from forming in the first place. The lines are coated with a special durable material that is very slippery when cold, making it difficult for ice to stick. More importantly, tiny sensors are attached along the lines to constantly monitor the weather conditions, specifically temperature and humidity. These sensors are connected to a control unit.

When the sensors detect that the air is cold enough for ice to form and the humidity is high, the system activates for a short period. It sends a very brief, low-power electrical pulse through the power line itself. This pulse is so short it doesn't affect the electricity supply, but it creates just enough gentle warmth to prevent the moisture in the air from freezing onto the line. As soon as the conditions are no longer right for ice, the system turns off completely. This way, energy is only used when there is an actual risk of ice buildup. The system stays dormant during warm weather, ensuring no energy is wasted. This approach stops ice at the very start, preventing heavy, dangerous loads from ever developing.

This works because it acts preemptively. Instead of trying to melt thick ice, which requires a lot of energy, the system adds a small amount of heat at the exact moment when water droplets are about to freeze. This slight change in temperature is enough to keep the water in a liquid state, so it simply drips off the line instead of turning into ice. The slippery coating provides a backup, making sure any ice that might start to form has a weak grip and is easily shed.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P35 Parameter Changes
outputs.text: Power lines can be protected from ice buildup by applying a special coating that changes its properties with temperature. This coating is a clear, durable material that is slightly soft and flexible when the weather is above freezing. This flexibility allows it to withstand normal conditions without cracking or peeling. However, when the temperature drops to freezing or below, this coating becomes much harder and more brittle. As ice starts to form on the line, the brittle coating helps to break the ice layer into smaller pieces, which then fall off naturally due to wind or the line's own movement. This prevents heavy ice from accumulating and damaging the lines, all without needing to heat the lines or interrupt power. The coating is also designed to be safe for electricity and long-lasting.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P35 Parameter Changes
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, a special coating made of phase-change material (PCM) can be applied to the lines. This material absorbs heat from the environment when temperatures drop below freezing, storing this energy as latent heat. When the temperature rises above freezing, the PCM releases the stored heat, warming the surface of the power lines and preventing ice from forming. This system is self-regulating, activating only when the temperature conditions are right for ice formation, thus conserving energy. The PCM coating uses natural temperature changes to keep the power lines above freezing without needing an external energy source. This efficient method prevents ice buildup, ensuring the power lines remain intact and functional without interrupting the electricity supply or wasting energy.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P36 Phase Transitions
outputs.text: Install automated monitoring systems on power transmission lines that detect ice accumulation through sensors measuring line weight, sag, or local weather conditions including temperature and humidity. When ice buildup is detected, the system temporarily increases the electrical current flowing through the affected line section. This can be accomplished by rerouting power through the line or utilizing the conductor's natural electrical resistance. The increased current generates heat through resistive heating, warming the metal conductor. This heat melts the thin layer of ice directly touching the conductor surface, creating a water interface between the metal and the ice sleeve. Once this bond is broken, the accumulated ice slides off the line under its own weight and falls safely to the ground. The system activates only when sensors confirm ice is actually present, preventing energy waste during warmer weather when no ice can form. The current increase is carefully controlled within safe operational limits and coordinated across the electrical grid to maintain continuous power delivery to customers throughout the de-icing process.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P36 Phase Transitions
outputs.text: A special material is applied to the surface of the power line or integrated into its outer layer. This material contains a salt solution that remains liquid even when the temperature drops below freezing. When ice starts to form on the line, the ice crystals act as a trigger, causing the solution to freeze. As it freezes, the material releases a small amount of heat, which is enough to melt the ice immediately around it, preventing a heavy buildup. Once the ice is gone, the process stops. When the weather warms up above freezing, the material naturally returns to its liquid state by absorbing heat from the surrounding air, ready for the next cold spell. This cycle happens automatically without any need for external power or controls. The system only activates when ice is present, so it does not waste energy when conditions are not icy.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P36 Phase Transitions
outputs.text: Power transmission lines can be protected from ice buildup by applying a specialized coating. This coating is a smart material that contains tiny capsules of a substance designed to melt at a temperature just above freezing, like 1-2 degrees Celsius. When the air temperature rises even slightly above freezing, or when the sun shines on the lines, this encapsulated substance absorbs heat from the environment and changes from a solid to a liquid. This process, called a phase change, uses up a lot of energy, which causes the surface of the power line to warm up just enough to melt the thin layer of ice that is bonded to it. As the ice's grip loosens, the coating itself can also be designed to subtly change its shape or expand slightly as it warms, further helping to break the ice free so it falls off. This system only activates when conditions are right for ice to melt, preventing unnecessary heating and ensuring the lines remain clear without needing to turn off the power.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P36 Phase Transitions
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, a closed-loop system containing a refrigerant with a low boiling point is integrated into the lines. This system uses the refrigerant's phase transition properties to manage ice formation. When temperatures approach freezing, the refrigerant absorbs heat from the environment as it evaporates, warming the lines and preventing ice from forming. The system is equipped with sensors that activate the refrigerant circulation only when necessary, ensuring energy efficiency. Additionally, the system can reverse the process, condensing the refrigerant back to liquid, which releases heat to help melt any existing ice. This method efficiently utilizes the natural heat absorption and release during phase transitions, providing a targeted solution to ice buildup without wasting energy during warmer conditions.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P37 Thermal Expansion
outputs.text:
Install power transmission lines using a composite conductor design with two key components: standard aluminum outer strands surrounding an inner core made from a specially selected metal alloy. These materials are chosen specifically because they expand at different rates when heated.

During icy conditions, the power line carries its normal electrical current, which generates heat through natural resistance. As the conductor warms even slightly, the inner core expands faster than the outer aluminum strands. This creates outward mechanical pressure and stress at the boundary where ice adheres to the metal surface. The stress cracks and breaks the bond between the ice and the conductor, causing the ice to fall away under its own weight.

During warmer weather above freezing, the temperature difference isn't large enough to trigger significant expansion mismatch, so the system remains dormant and wastes no energy. The solution is entirely passive and automatic, requiring no sensors, controls, or operator intervention. It activates naturally whenever ice forms and current flows, providing continuous protection without interrupting electrical service.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P37 Thermal Expansion
outputs.text: A solution involves integrating a thin, conductive layer of aluminum within the steel core of the power transmission line. When temperatures fall below freezing and ice begins to form, sensors detect the conditions and activate a low-level electrical current through the aluminum layer. This current gently heats the aluminum, causing it to expand more than the surrounding steel due to its higher thermal expansion coefficient. The difference in expansion creates mechanical stress at the point where ice adheres to the line, effectively cracking and shedding the ice without damaging the cable. The system only operates when freezing occurs and ice is present, ensuring that no energy is wasted during warmer weather. This approach keeps the lines clear, maintains uninterrupted power flow, and uses minimal electricity targeted precisely when needed.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P37 Thermal Expansion
outputs.text: Power lines can be designed with a special outer layer that expands more than the inner part when heated. When ice starts to form, a quick burst of extra electricity is sent through the line. This makes the line warm up slightly and very quickly. Because the outer layer expands more than the inner part, it creates a tiny but powerful stretching and cracking motion right where the ice is stuck. This action breaks the ice away from the line, causing it to fall off. Once the ice is gone, the extra electricity stops, and the line cools back down to its normal temperature. This way, the lines are only heated when ice is actually present, saving energy and keeping the power flowing without interruption.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P37 Thermal Expansion
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on power-transmission lines, a bimetallic strip can be attached along the length of the lines. This strip is made of two metals with different thermal expansion rates. When temperatures drop and ice starts to form, the strip bends due to the metals expanding at different rates. This bending action causes the power line to shake or vibrate, dislodging the ice. This method is efficient because it uses the natural temperature changes to activate the ice-removal process, eliminating the need for continuous heating. When temperatures are above freezing, the strip remains straight, ensuring no energy is wasted. This solution keeps the lines clear of ice without interrupting the electricity supply or using unnecessary energy.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P38 Strong Oxidants
outputs.text:
Install corona discharge devices at intervals along power transmission lines in ice-prone areas. These devices harness the strong electrical field already present around high-voltage lines to create controlled electrical discharges in the surrounding air. When ice detection sensors identify conditions where ice is forming—through measurements of line tension, sag, or environmental conditions—the system activates automatically.

The corona discharge ionizes air molecules near the conductor surface, creating charged particles and reactive oxygen compounds. As these ions recombine and react, they release energy as heat directly at the conductor surface where ice adheres. This localized heating melts the ice-conductor bond, allowing ice to fall away safely.

Temperature and humidity sensors ensure the system operates only when ice can actually form, keeping it dormant during warmer weather to avoid wasting energy. The devices draw power directly from the transmission line itself, requiring no separate power source. This approach removes dangerous ice accumulation while maintaining continuous electrical service and minimizing energy consumption during ice-free periods.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P38 Strong Oxidants
outputs.text:
A system is installed on the power transmission infrastructure that removes ice by using ozone gas. Small, durable dispensers are mounted at intervals along the power lines. These dispensers are connected to a central ozone generator. Sensors on the lines continuously monitor for the formation of ice. When ice is detected, the system activates and releases a controlled amount of ozone gas directly onto the icy sections of the cable.

Ozone is a highly reactive form of oxygen. When it comes into contact with the ice, a chemical reaction occurs. This reaction releases heat, which melts the ice at the point of contact. The reaction is localized and efficient, focusing energy only where the ice has built up. Once the ice is melted and the sensors detect clear lines, the ozone release stops. The ozone itself naturally breaks down into harmless oxygen after the reaction is complete. This approach ensures that energy is expended only when there is actual ice to remove, avoiding the constant energy drain of heating the lines during warm conditions. The system provides a reliable, on-demand de-icing method that protects the lines without interrupting the flow of electricity.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P38 Strong Oxidants
outputs.text: Power transmission lines can be coated with a special material that actively prevents ice from forming. This coating, similar to those used in self-cleaning windows, uses sunlight to trigger a chemical reaction on its surface. When ultraviolet light from the sun hits the coating, it reacts with moisture and oxygen in the air to create tiny, highly reactive molecules. These molecules then break down any water or ice that tries to stick to the line, effectively melting or preventing its formation at a microscopic level. This process only happens when there's enough sunlight and moisture, so it doesn't waste energy when it's warm and dry. The ice simply cannot accumulate, keeping the lines clear and safe without needing to turn off the power.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P38 Strong Oxidants
outputs.text: To prevent ice buildup on overhead power-transmission lines, install ozone generators along the lines. These generators will produce ozone, which is a strong oxidant capable of lowering the freezing point of water and breaking down ice. The ozone can be directed onto the lines during conditions that are likely to lead to ice formation. This approach prevents ice from accumulating or helps remove existing ice without the need for heating the lines. The system is energy-efficient because it only activates when necessary, based on weather conditions, and does not require continuous energy consumption like traditional heating methods. Ozone's ability to interact with ice reduces its adhesion to the lines, facilitating its removal and preventing damage to the power infrastructure.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P39 Inert Atmosphere
outputs.text:
Install a system that sprays a fine mist of food-grade propylene glycol antifreeze onto power transmission lines when temperatures approach freezing and humidity is high. Temperature and humidity sensors mounted on transmission towers monitor weather conditions continuously and automatically activate spray nozzles only when icing conditions are imminent—typically when temperatures drop below 2°C with high moisture in the air.

The propylene glycol creates a thin protective coating on the power lines that prevents ice from bonding to the metal surface. Because propylene glycol is non-conductive, it doesn't interfere with electricity transmission. The system uses minimal energy to pump and atomize the antifreeze solution, consuming far less power than heating the lines would require. The coating naturally drips off over time and biodegrades, so it requires periodic reapplication only during continued freezing conditions.

This approach prevents ice accumulation before it starts rather than removing ice after it forms, eliminating the weight problem while maintaining continuous power delivery and operating only when actually needed.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P39 Inert Atmosphere
outputs.text: Enclose the overhead power lines in a durable, transparent sheath that is sealed and filled with a dry, inert gas, such as nitrogen, at low pressure. This sheath surrounds the entire length of the line, creating a protective barrier that prevents moisture from reaching the cable surface. Because the environment inside is dry and inert, ice cannot form or stick to the line even in freezing conditions. The sheath is also designed to provide thermal insulation, reducing heat loss from the line. Importantly, the transparency allows sunlight to pass through without unnecessary absorption, so the system does not cause overheating when temperatures are above freezing. The power line continues to conduct electricity normally inside this enclosure, so there is no interruption to the power supply. This approach requires no active heating or energy input, operating passively to keep the lines ice-free throughout cold weather.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P39 Inert Atmosphere
outputs.text: Overhead power transmission lines can be protected from ice buildup by enclosing them within a continuous, flexible, and transparent outer casing. This casing would be made from a durable, non-conductive material and would be filled with a specialized, non-flammable gas that does not conduct electricity. The gas would be sealed inside the casing at a slightly higher pressure than the surrounding air. This protective gas layer would prevent any moisture from directly touching the power line itself, thereby stopping ice from forming on its surface. The transparent casing would allow maintenance crews to visually inspect the line. This method ensures that ice cannot accumulate, eliminating the risk of damage and the need for energy-intensive de-icing, while keeping the electricity flowing without interruption.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P39 Inert Atmosphere
outputs.text: To prevent ice from forming on overhead power-transmission lines, a protective sheath filled with an inert gas like argon can be installed around the lines. This sheath acts as a barrier, preventing moisture from reaching the lines and thus stopping ice from forming. The inert gas inside the sheath does not react with moisture, creating a stable environment that inhibits ice buildup. The sheath can be equipped with sensors to monitor weather conditions, activating the inert gas deployment only when necessary. This ensures that the system operates efficiently, conserving energy by only functioning when ice formation is likely. This approach maintains the integrity of the power supply without the need for energy-intensive heating, effectively preventing ice buildup and potential damage to the lines.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P40 Composite Materials
outputs.text:
Install power transmission lines made from a composite cable structure combining two functional layers. The inner core consists of traditional aluminum or copper conductor that carries electricity efficiently. Surrounding this core is an outer sheath containing shape-memory alloy wires woven into the cable structure.

When ice accumulates on the lines, sensors detect the added weight or icing conditions. A small pulsed electrical current is then routed through the shape-memory alloy wires, causing them to heat slightly and contract repeatedly. This creates mechanical vibrations and flexing motions that crack the ice buildup, causing it to fall away from the cable.

The system operates independently from the main power transmission, so electricity supply continues uninterrupted during ice removal. Because the shape-memory alloy removes ice through mechanical action rather than melting it entirely, the energy required is minimal compared to heating the whole cable. The system activates only when ice is detected, preventing energy waste during warmer weather. This composite cable design provides both reliable power transmission and efficient, on-demand ice removal in a single integrated structure.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P40 Composite Materials
outputs.text: The overhead power lines are coated with a thin, durable composite material made of three key components: a conductive outer mesh that allows electricity to pass through unimpeded, a middle layer of water-repellent polymer that prevents water droplets from sticking and freezing solidly, and a smooth, low-friction surface that reduces ice adhesion. When sensors detect ice beginning to form, a very small electrical current is sent through the conductive layer, generating just enough heat to loosen the ice, which then slides off due to the slippery coating. This system only activates during icy conditions, so it doesn't waste energy when the weather is warm. The composite material ensures that the lines remain functional, ice-free, and energy-efficient, combining conductivity, water resistance, and easy ice release in one smart coating.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P40 Composite Materials
outputs.text: Power lines can be made with a special outer coating. The inside of the line would still be made of highly conductive metal, like aluminum, to carry electricity efficiently. This metal core would then be covered with a very thin, clear outer layer. This outer layer is designed to be extremely water-repellent, similar to how water beads up and rolls off a lotus leaf. It would have a microscopic texture or special materials embedded within it that prevent water droplets from sticking. When moisture or freezing rain hits the line, it wouldn't be able to freeze and bond strongly to the surface. Instead, the water would form tiny beads that are easily blown away by wind or dislodged by the natural swaying of the line, preventing ice from building up in the first place. This keeps the lines clear without needing to heat them.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P40 Composite Materials
outputs.text: The solution involves using a composite material for power-transmission lines that combines a strong core, like carbon fiber, with a smart material layer, such as a shape-memory alloy or thermally responsive polymer. This smart layer reacts to freezing temperatures by contracting or expanding, causing the line to vibrate slightly. This vibration effectively shakes off any ice that starts to form, preventing heavy buildup. The composite design ensures the lines remain strong and durable while providing an automatic, energy-efficient method of ice removal that activates only when temperatures drop to freezing. This approach eliminates the need for continuous heating, conserving energy and maintaining uninterrupted electricity supply.

problems.problem: A confectionery factory produces chocolates with a liquid sugar-syrup (liqueur) centre. To raise output the liquid filling is warmed so it flows freely and can be dispensed quickly into the moulds, but the hot filling softens and melts the chocolate shells it is meant to sit inside. The factory needs much higher filling throughput without the heat ruining the chocolate.
problems.source: casebase.json P03 (patent-derived); original source in notes

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text:
Heat the liquid filling in a reservoir to reduce its viscosity for fast flow, but install a compact heat exchanger in the dispensing line immediately before the nozzle. As the warm syrup flows toward the molds, it passes through a cooling section—either a jacketed tube with chilled water circulation or a coil surrounded by coolant. This rapidly drops the syrup temperature to just above the chocolate's melting point (around 30-32°C) in the final moment before dispensing.

The syrup maintains sufficient fluidity during the brief dispensing time due to its momentum and the short duration of flow, allowing quick filling. However, the cooled syrup no longer carries enough heat to soften or melt the chocolate shells. The factory achieves high throughput by keeping the bulk syrup hot for low viscosity throughout the supply system, while protecting the chocolate through last-second cooling at the point of contact.

**Why it works:** The solution exploits the time delay between cooling and viscosity increase. The syrup cools just before dispensing but doesn't have time to thicken significantly during the split-second filling operation, maintaining flow speed while eliminating heat damage to the chocolate.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: Pre-cool the chocolate shells before filling them. The shells should be chilled to a temperature well below their normal melting point, for example, in a refrigerated chamber or on a cooled conveyor belt. This makes the chocolate shells very cold and thermally stable. The liquid sugar-syrup filling can still be warmed to a temperature that keeps it flowing freely for fast dispensing. When the warm syrup is injected into the cold shell, the cold chocolate absorbs the heat from the syrup. This prevents the chocolate from softening or melting because its temperature stays safely low. The syrup itself will then cool and begin to set rapidly upon contact. This method allows for a much faster filling throughput because the syrup remains fluid during dispensing, but the chocolate is protected by its initial cold state. The factory can implement this using standard industrial cooling systems integrated before the filling station in the production line.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: The factory should implement a two-stage filling process. First, the warm, free-flowing sugar syrup is dispensed into the chocolate shells. Immediately after filling, the chocolates are passed through a rapid cooling tunnel, using chilled air or a refrigerated conveyor belt, to quickly solidify the outer layer of the syrup. This rapid cooling prevents the heat from the syrup from penetrating deeply into the chocolate shell and causing it to melt. Once this outer layer of syrup has solidified, the chocolates can then proceed to a second, slower cooling stage to fully set the entire syrup center and the chocolate shell. This method allows for high-speed dispensing of warm syrup while preserving the integrity of the chocolate.

outputs.label: TRIZ off (baseline)
outputs.text: To prevent the chocolate shells from melting when filled with warm liquid, the factory should implement a two-step cooling and filling process. First, pre-cool the chocolate shells by placing them in a refrigerated environment or using a cooling tunnel to lower their temperature. This ensures the shells have a greater thermal capacity to absorb heat from the filling without melting. Second, adjust the filling process to use a cooled nozzle or dispensing system. This system should cool the liquid filling just before it enters the chocolate shell, reducing its temperature to a level that remains fluid for quick dispensing but not hot enough to melt the chocolate. This can be achieved by integrating a heat exchanger or cooling jacket around the dispensing nozzle. By pre-cooling the shells and using a cooled dispensing system, the factory can maintain the integrity of the chocolate shells while increasing the throughput of the liquid filling process, ensuring higher production rates without compromising product quality.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text:
Pre-cool the empty chocolate shells to several degrees below room temperature (around 10-15°C) using a refrigerated staging area before the filling operation. Then dispense the warmed, free-flowing liquid filling into these cold shells at normal high speed. The significantly cooled chocolate shells act as a heat sink that absorbs the thermal energy from the hot filling without the chocolate itself reaching its melting point.

The cold chocolate provides a thermal buffer period during which the hot filling can be quickly dispensed. As the filling makes contact with the cold shell, it immediately begins cooling and losing fluidity, while the chocolate gradually warms but stays well below its melting temperature. After filling, the chocolates proceed through normal production, with both the filling and shell equilibrating to room temperature naturally. This approach maintains high throughput with hot, fast-flowing filling while protecting the chocolate shells from heat damage through strategic temperature management.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: The solution is to deeply freeze the empty chocolate shells on the production line immediately before they are filled. As the conveyor carries the shells to the filling station, they pass through a powerful, focused cooling unit, such as a blast chiller using very cold air or nitrogen. This rapidly chills the chocolate shells to a temperature far below their normal state, making them hard and brittle. When the warm, liquid syrup is then injected at high speed, the intense cold of the shell acts as a heat sink, instantly absorbing the thermal energy. The outer part of the shell remains solid, while the inner surface may melt just enough to create a perfect seal with the filling. The chocolate then proceeds to the standard cooling tunnel, where it returns to a stable, uniform temperature. This method allows the factory to use a much hotter, faster-flowing filling because the pre-cooled shell can absorb the heat without losing its structural integrity.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: The factory will implement a two-stage process for filling the chocolates. First, the chocolate shells will be significantly pre-chilled to a very low temperature, just above freezing, before they reach the filling station. This makes them much more resistant to heat. Second, the liquid sugar-syrup will be kept at a moderate, warm temperature to prevent it from solidifying. Immediately before it is dispensed into the chilled chocolate shells, the syrup will pass through a very short, intense heating zone, such as a microwave or induction coil. This brief, powerful burst of heat will rapidly reduce the syrup's viscosity, allowing it to flow extremely quickly into the molds. The pre-chilled chocolate will then quickly absorb the heat from the syrup, preventing any melting and allowing for a much faster production rate.

outputs.label: TRIZ on (keyword only)
outputs.text: To prevent the chocolate shells from melting while increasing the throughput of the liquid filling, the factory should implement a two-step process. First, the chocolate shells are pre-cooled to a lower temperature than usual by placing them in a refrigerated environment or using a cooling tunnel. This makes the shells more resistant to the heat of the liquid filling. Next, instead of heating the entire liquid filling, only a small portion is heated to the desired temperature for quick dispensing. This heated portion is then mixed with the rest of the filling, which remains at a cooler temperature. This method ensures that the overall temperature of the filling is lower, reducing the risk of melting the chocolate shells while maintaining a high flow rate for quick dispensing. By reducing the temperature differential between the filling and the chocolate shells, the factory can achieve higher throughput without compromising the quality of the chocolates.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text: Create a thin frozen shell around each portion of hot liquid filling before placing it into the chocolate molds. First, dispense the hot liquid filling onto a very cold surface or into small chilled cavities. This flash-freezes only the outer surface of each portion, creating small capsules with a frozen exterior and liquid interior. Immediately transfer these frozen capsules into the waiting chocolate shells. The frozen outer layer acts as a protective barrier, preventing the hot liquid center from directly touching and melting the chocolate. The filling can still be heated for fast, efficient dispensing, maintaining high production speeds. Once inside the chocolate shell, the frozen exterior quickly melts at room temperature, blending seamlessly with the liquid core to create the desired uniform liquid center. This approach allows the factory to keep the filling hot for rapid throughput while protecting the delicate chocolate shells from heat damage during the critical moment of contact.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text: The chocolate shells are briefly cooled right before the liquid filling is added. A quick blast of chilled air or a cold plate contact hardens the inner surface of each shell, making it resistant to melting. The sugar-syrup filling is kept warm to ensure it flows easily and can be dispensed rapidly into the moulds. The momentarily cooled shell can handle the brief exposure to the warm liquid without softening, as the local cooling provides a buffer against the heat. This allows the factory to use a hotter, faster-flowing filling without damaging the chocolate. The cooling step is integrated into the production line just prior to filling, ensuring each shell is treated individually and efficiently. After filling, the shells return to ambient temperature, and the filling sets normally. This method increases throughput by enabling faster dispensing while preserving product quality.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text:
The chocolate shells will be rapidly pre-chilled to a very low temperature immediately before the warm liquid sugar-syrup filling is dispensed. This can be achieved by passing the empty chocolate shells through a localized zone of extremely cold air or a fine mist of liquid nitrogen vapor. The intense cold will create a temporary, super-cooled inner surface on the chocolate, allowing it to absorb the heat from the warm syrup without melting. The syrup will then quickly cool and solidify within the chilled shell, maintaining the chocolate's integrity and shape. This process ensures high throughput by allowing the syrup to remain warm and free-flowing, while preventing any damage to the delicate chocolate.

This solution works because it addresses the heat transfer problem by creating a significant temperature differential in the opposite direction. By making the chocolate shell much colder, it can absorb the heat from the warm syrup without reaching its melting point, effectively isolating the heat of the syrup from the structural integrity of the chocolate.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + all-40 attachment
outputs.text: To prevent the chocolate shells from melting when filled with hot liquid syrup, the factory should implement a multi-step approach. First, the chocolate moulds should be cooled using a refrigerated environment or a cooling conveyor belt. This ensures the chocolate shells are more resistant to the heat of the filling. Next, the filling process should be accelerated by using a pneumatic system to dispense the syrup quickly, minimizing the time the hot liquid is in contact with the chocolate. Additionally, an edible barrier can be introduced between the chocolate shell and the filling. This barrier, made from a material that melts at a higher temperature than chocolate, acts as insulation, preventing the heat from the syrup from directly affecting the chocolate. By combining these methods, the factory can increase throughput without compromising the integrity of the chocolate shells.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text:
Install a specialized filling system that heats the liqueur in very small batches just moments before dispensing, rather than warming large quantities. The bulk storage tank keeps the filling at room temperature. A pump draws small amounts (enough for 10-20 chocolates) through a compact inline heater that rapidly warms the liquid to flowing temperature. This hot portion immediately flows through a heated nozzle into the waiting chocolate shells.

The key innovation is what happens next: immediately after each portion is dispensed into the moulds, a fine mist of food-grade cooling agent (chilled alcohol vapor or carbon dioxide) sprays directly onto the filling surface, or a focused cold air jet chills it. This rapid cooling creates a protective cooled layer on the filling within seconds.

The system processes the liqueur through distinct temperature stages: cold storage, instant micro-batch heating, quick dispensing while hot, then immediate surface cooling. The filling stays hot only briefly during its journey through the dispensing nozzle, flowing freely for fast production. By the time it could transfer significant heat to the chocolate shell, the cooling system has already dropped its surface temperature to safe levels, protecting the chocolate from melting.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text: The solution involves creating the liquid filling in two separate stages to keep the heat away from the chocolate shells. First, the warm, free-flowing sugar syrup is dispensed not directly into the chocolate, but into many small, disposable moulds to form individual solid pellets. These pellets have a thin, hard outer shell made from an edible material like a special sugar or hardened fat that acts as a thermal insulator. The pellets are then cooled quickly so their outside is at a safe, cool temperature. In a second, entirely separate step on the production line, these pre-filled, cool pellets are mechanically placed into the waiting chocolate shells. The chocolate is never exposed to the hot liquid because it only comes into contact with the cool exterior of the pellet. This allows the factory to heat the syrup as much as needed to achieve very fast dispensing speeds for making the pellets, without any risk of the heat damaging the final chocolate product. The high throughput is achieved in the pellet-making stage, while the assembly stage remains cool and safe for the chocolate.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text:
The factory should implement a system where the bulk of the liquid sugar-syrup filling is maintained at a temperature that is cool enough not to damage the chocolate shells. Immediately before dispensing, the syrup will pass through a very small, highly localized, and rapid heating element positioned directly at the nozzle. This element will instantly raise the temperature of only the tiny volume of syrup being dispensed at that precise moment, making it flow freely. The heated syrup will then be quickly injected into the chocolate mould. This ensures that the vast majority of the syrup remains cool, and the chocolate shell is only exposed to the hot, free-flowing syrup for a fleeting instant, preventing it from melting.

This works because it isolates the heating process to only the exact moment and location of dispensing. The chocolate shell is exposed to the hot liquid for such a short duration that significant heat transfer and melting are avoided, while the syrup still achieves the necessary flow rate for high-speed production.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P01 Segmentation
outputs.text: The solution involves modifying the filling process by dividing it into multiple stages. Instead of filling the chocolate shell with the hot liquid all at once, the liquid is dispensed in small, controlled layers. Each layer is allowed to cool slightly before the next one is added, reducing the heat impact on the chocolate shell and preventing it from melting. To further enhance this process, a segmented nozzle is used to dispense the liquid in thin streams or droplets, increasing the surface area for faster cooling. This approach maintains the integrity of the chocolate shell while allowing for a high throughput of the liquid filling process. By segmenting the filling process and using a specialized nozzle, the factory can achieve higher production rates without compromising the quality of the chocolates.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: Install a rapid cooling system directly at the dispensing nozzle. Keep the liquid filling heated in the storage tanks and delivery pipes so it flows quickly and freely, maintaining high dispensing speed. However, as the filling exits the nozzle and travels toward the chocolate shell, pass it through a flash-cooling zone. This can be achieved by surrounding the dispensing path with a burst of cryogenic gas such as liquid nitrogen vapor or carbon dioxide, or by using a focused stream of extremely cold air. The cooling happens in a fraction of a second, instantly chilling the outer surface of the filling droplet. By the time the filling lands in the chocolate shell, it has cooled to a safe temperature that won't melt the chocolate. The filling benefits from being hot during storage and flow (enabling fast dispensing), but arrives cool where it matters (at contact with chocolate). This maintains high production throughput while protecting the chocolate shells from heat damage.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: The solution involves installing a high-speed, precision dispensing system that heats the liquid sugar-syrup filling to a high temperature within the dispensing nozzle itself, just milliseconds before it is injected into the chocolate shell. The filling travels through an insulated pipe to the nozzle, where a small, integrated heating element rapidly brings it to the ideal temperature for fast flow. The actual injection process is extremely quick, with the nozzle designed to release the exact amount of filling in a fraction of a second and then retract immediately. This minimizes the contact time between the hot liquid and the delicate chocolate. The chocolate shell, being a poor conductor of heat, does not have enough time to absorb a significant amount of thermal energy before the filling is already inside and begins to cool. The entire filling station is kept in a cool environment to help the chocolate shells maintain their integrity. After injection, the filled chocolates move on a conveyor belt through a cooling tunnel where the liquid centre sets quickly. This method allows the factory to use a much warmer, free-flowing filling to achieve high throughput, while the brief exposure prevents the chocolate from melting and ruining the product.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: The factory should implement a two-stage dispensing system for the liquid sugar-syrup. The syrup will be heated in a main reservoir to achieve optimal flow rates for high-speed dispensing. Immediately before the syrup is injected into the chocolate moulds, it will pass through a specialized nozzle equipped with a rapid, localized cooling mechanism, such as a small, chilled jacket or coil. This cooling mechanism will quickly reduce the temperature of only the outermost layer of the syrup stream as it exits the nozzle. This creates a thin, cooler "skin" around the still-hot core of the syrup. This cooler outer layer acts as a temporary thermal barrier, preventing the immediate melting or softening of the chocolate shell upon contact. The bulk of the syrup inside remains hot, ensuring it flows freely and quickly, maintaining the desired high production throughput without damaging the chocolate.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P02 Separation
outputs.text: The solution involves using a dual-chamber dispensing system for the liquid filling. The main chamber holds the liquid filling at room temperature, ensuring it remains cool and does not affect the chocolate shells. A secondary, smaller chamber rapidly heats a small portion of the filling just before it is dispensed. This heated portion is then mixed with the room temperature filling immediately before being injected into the chocolate shells. This method allows the filling to flow quickly and efficiently without raising the overall temperature enough to melt the chocolate shells. By separating the heating process from the filling process, the factory can achieve higher throughput without compromising the integrity of the chocolate shells. This approach ensures that the chocolate shells remain intact while still allowing for a fast and efficient filling process.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text: Install a specialized dispensing system that heats the liqueur filling only at the moment of dispensing. The bulk of the filling remains stored at cool room temperature. Just before the dispensing nozzle, a small heating chamber uses rapid heating technology (such as induction heating or a compact heat exchanger) to instantly warm only the tiny amount of filling about to be dispensed—perhaps just a few seconds' worth at a time. This heated filling flows quickly and freely through the nozzle. The nozzle tip itself incorporates a cooling jacket with chilled water or refrigerant circulating through it, so the filling cools immediately as it exits and enters the chocolate shell. This approach creates different temperature zones: cool storage, a brief hot zone for flow, and immediate cooling at delivery. The filling achieves the necessary fluidity for rapid dispensing while spending so little time at elevated temperature that it doesn't melt or soften the chocolate shells, allowing the factory to dramatically increase production throughput safely.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text: The solution involves modifying the chocolate moulds to include a built-in cooling system that chills only the inner surface of each chocolate shell just before and during the filling process. Each mould cavity is fabricated with very fine channels running just behind its inner wall. A chilled, food-safe liquid is pumped through these channels for a short time when the warm liquid filling is injected. This quick local cooling causes a thin layer of the chocolate shell's interior to solidify and become resistant to melting. The hot syrup can thus be dispensed rapidly without softening the shell. After filling, the cooling liquid stops flowing, allowing the chocolate to return to a uniform temperature. The moulds are designed so that the cooling is extremely localized, targeting only the contact surface between the filling and the shell, and does not affect the outer part of the chocolate or its appearance. This method allows the factory to use a hotter, faster-flowing filling while ensuring the chocolate shells remain intact and firm. The cooling is applied precisely when and where it is needed—during the filling operation—and then removed, so the finished chocolate has normal texture and taste.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text: The chocolate factory can install specialized dispensing nozzles for the liquid filling. These nozzles will have a clever design that, as the warm liquid sugar syrup flows out, a very thin, cool layer is created around its outside edge. This cool layer acts like a protective shield, forming just before the liquid touches the chocolate shell. The main part of the syrup stays warm and runny, allowing it to be dispensed quickly, but the cool outer skin prevents it from melting the delicate chocolate. This way, the factory can fill chocolates much faster without any damage, ensuring perfect liqueur centres every time.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P03 Local Quality
outputs.text: The solution involves creating a chocolate shell with two distinct layers, each designed for specific thermal properties. The outer layer of the shell is made thicker and from a chocolate blend with a higher melting point, which acts as a protective barrier against the heat of the liquid filling. This ensures that the outer shell remains solid and intact during the filling process. The inner layer is thinner and made from a chocolate with a lower melting point, preserving the desired taste and texture when in contact with the filling. Additionally, the filling is dispensed through a specially designed nozzle that cools the liquid slightly before it enters the shell. This cooling step reduces the temperature of the filling just enough to prevent it from melting the chocolate, while still allowing for a high flow rate to increase production speed. This method effectively balances the need for rapid filling with the requirement to maintain the quality and structure of the chocolate shells.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text:
Install a specialized two-stage dispensing nozzle that heats and cools the liquid filling at different points in its path. The upper section of the nozzle contains heating elements that keep the syrup warm as it flows through the supply channel, maintaining low viscosity for rapid dispensing. Just before the syrup exits into the chocolate shells, it passes through a cooling section at the nozzle tip, where refrigerant channels rapidly drop the temperature to a safe level that won't melt the chocolate.

The key is creating different temperature zones along the flow path rather than treating the filling uniformly. The syrup stays hot where speed matters—in the supply lines and upper nozzle—but becomes cool exactly where it contacts the chocolate. This asymmetric temperature profile allows the factory to maintain fast flow rates throughout most of the system while protecting the delicate chocolate shells at the critical moment of filling. The result is high throughput without thermal damage.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text: The solution is to create a brief, localized cooling effect on the inside of each chocolate shell right before the warm liquid filling is added. As each empty chocolate shell moves into position under the filling nozzle, a tiny, precise jet of chilled air or a fine food-safe mist is directed into its center cavity for a fraction of a second. This quickly solidifies a very thin extra layer on the inner surface of the chocolate. The warm syrup is then injected normally at high speed. This thin, newly hardened lining acts as a thermal shield. It absorbs the initial heat from the filling without letting that heat travel into and soften the main body of the chocolate shell. The filling remains hot enough to flow rapidly for high output, but the chocolate retains its shape and integrity because only its interior surface was temporarily and asymmetrically cooled. The rest of the shell stays at its ideal, stable temperature.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text:
The factory will implement a new filling nozzle design. This nozzle will have a specialized outer ring that rapidly cools the very surface of the liquid sugar-syrup just as it exits the nozzle. The main body of the syrup inside the nozzle will remain warm and free-flowing, ensuring quick dispensing. However, the thin, cooled outer layer of the syrup will act as a protective barrier, preventing the hot interior from directly touching and melting the chocolate shell. This allows for fast filling without damaging the delicate chocolate. The cooled outer layer will quickly solidify upon contact with the chocolate, further strengthening the shell and preventing leaks, while the inner syrup remains liquid.

This works because the asymmetry in temperature distribution within the liquid filling itself creates a protective layer. The hot core maintains high flow rates, while the rapidly cooled outer "skin" of the filling acts as an immediate thermal buffer, preventing the heat from reaching and melting the chocolate shell.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P04 Asymmetry
outputs.text: To prevent the hot liquid filling from melting the chocolate shells, the chocolate moulds should be redesigned to be asymmetrical. The moulds will have thicker walls on the side that first receives the liquid filling, acting as an insulator to absorb and dissipate heat more effectively. This thicker side will prevent the chocolate from melting when the hot filling is dispensed. Additionally, the base of the moulds will be sloped or angled, allowing the liquid filling to flow quickly to the cooler, thinner side, where it can settle without damaging the chocolate shell. This design ensures that the chocolate shell remains intact while allowing for a faster filling process. By creating a thermal barrier with the thicker side and facilitating faster filling with the sloped base, the chocolate shells are protected from the heat, enabling higher throughput without compromising the quality of the chocolates.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text: Install a specialized dispensing system where the liquid filling travels through heated lines to maintain fast, free-flowing delivery at elevated temperature. At the dispensing nozzle itself, add a cooling collar that injects compressed carbon dioxide or liquid nitrogen directly around the stream of hot filling as it exits. This flash-cooling happens in a fraction of a second, rapidly chilling the outer surface of the filling to form a protective cooled layer while the interior remains liquid and warm. The filling emerges flowing quickly due to its low viscosity from heating, but the outer surface has been cooled enough that it won't melt the chocolate shell upon contact. The system combines both heating for flow and cooling for protection in one continuous operation at the point of dispensing. This allows maximum throughput since the filling flows freely through the entire system until the last moment, when only its surface is cooled just before entering the chocolate mould.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text:
The solution is to cool the liquid filling at the exact moment it is dispensed into the chocolate shell. This is achieved by modifying the filling nozzle to mix the warm syrup with a pre-chilled, food-safe ingredient just before it exits. As the two streams merge inside the nozzle, the cold ingredient rapidly absorbs the heat from the syrup, causing the filling to thicken or partially set instantly upon entering the mould. The chocolate shell is therefore exposed only to a filling that is already cooling down, rather than a pool of hot liquid. This prevents the shell from softening or melting.

Another way to accomplish this is to design the filling as two separate, stable pastes that are not liquid on their own. These two pastes are pumped separately and merged together only at the tip of the dispensing nozzle. The chemical reaction or physical blending of these two components upon merging causes the mixture to become fluid for a very brief moment, allowing it to be injected easily, and then to solidify quickly. This quick setting action happens so fast that the chocolate does not have time to absorb enough heat to melt. The key is that the act of filling and the act of the filling solidifying are combined into one instantaneous step right inside the shell.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text:
The factory should implement a new dispensing system for the liquid sugar-syrup centers. This system will use specialized nozzles that incorporate both heating and rapid cooling elements. The sugar syrup will be stored at a moderate temperature in a main reservoir. As the syrup enters each dispensing nozzle, it will pass through a very short, intense heating zone, quickly raising its temperature to ensure it flows freely and rapidly. Immediately after this heating, and as the syrup exits the nozzle into the chocolate shell, the nozzle will simultaneously apply a rapid cooling effect directly to the exiting syrup stream. This cooling could be achieved using small, localized cold air jets or tiny thermoelectric coolers integrated into the nozzle tip. This ensures the syrup is hot enough for quick dispensing but instantly cools or solidifies upon contact with the chocolate, preventing any melting of the shell.

This solution works because it merges the heating and cooling operations in a tightly controlled, sequential manner within the dispensing process itself. The syrup is only hot for the briefest moment necessary for flow, and then immediately cooled as it enters the chocolate, effectively isolating the heat from the delicate shell.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P05 Merging
outputs.text: The solution involves using a dual-chamber nozzle system that simultaneously dispenses the liquid filling and a cooling agent into the chocolate moulds. As the warm liquid filling is released, a fine mist of cold air or a chilled inert gas like nitrogen is also introduced. This cooling agent rapidly reduces the temperature of the liquid filling as it enters the chocolate shell, preventing the shell from melting. The cooling agent can also create a thin insulating layer between the filling and the chocolate, providing additional protection against heat. By merging the filling and cooling processes, the factory can achieve a higher throughput of liquid filling without compromising the quality of the chocolate shells. This approach allows for quick dispensing of the filling while maintaining the structural integrity of the chocolates.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text:
Install specially designed double-walled dispensing nozzles that perform multiple functions simultaneously. Each nozzle has an inner channel that carries the hot liquid filling at optimal flow temperature for fast dispensing. Surrounding this inner channel is an outer jacket through which cryogenic coolant (such as liquid nitrogen or carbon dioxide) continuously circulates.

As the hot filling travels through the nozzle, it remains warm enough to flow quickly. However, in the final section of the nozzle—just before the liquid exits—the intense cold from the outer jacket flash-cools the filling in milliseconds. By the time the liquid contacts the chocolate shell, it has dropped to a safe temperature that won't melt or soften the chocolate.

This integrated nozzle eliminates the need for separate cooling equipment or slower filling processes. The factory maintains high throughput with hot filling upstream while protecting the chocolate shells at the point of contact. The same device handles heating, dispensing, and cooling in one compact unit.

outputs.label: TRIZ on + P06 Universality
outputs.text: The solution is to create a dual-layer chocolate shell where a thin, edible heat-resistant lining is applied inside the mould first. This lining acts as a thermal barrier. It could be made from a thin coating of a high-melting-point cocoa butter mixture or a very fine layer of a sugar-based glaze that solidifies quickly. After this lining sets, the regular chocolate shell is formed over it in the same mould. When the warm liquid syrup filling is dispensed at high speed, it contacts this inner barrier instead of the main chocolate directly. The barrier is designed to withstand the filling's temperature without melting itself and without transferring sufficient heat to melt the outer chocolate layer. This allows the factory to use a hotter, faster-flowing filling to increase throughput, while the chocolate shell retains its shape and texture. The entire product remains entirely edible, with the barrier being imperceptible in taste or texture in the final candy. The lining is applied using a quick spray or fine deposition step integrated into the existing moulding process, minimizing added complexity.
